message:
fix: correct paper year (ICLR 2026), add arXiv links, fix bits/dim claim

- Landing page: "Google Research, 2024" -> "Google Research, ICLR 2026"
- README: add arXiv link (2504.19874) to paper title and credits
- README: fix code comment "~3 bits/dim" -> "~4.5 bits/dim" to match quality section
- 3DGS page: clarify scene data sizes (158 MB + 65 MB)

files.patch:
@@ -7,7 +7,7 @@
 
 WASM + relaxed SIMD build of [botirk38/turboquant](https://github.com/botirk38/turboquant) for browsers and Node.js.
 
-Based on the paper "TurboQuant: Online Vector Quantization with Near-optimal Distortion Rate".
+Based on the paper ["TurboQuant: Online Vector Quantization with Near-optimal Distortion Rate"](https://arxiv.org/abs/2504.19874) (Google Research, ICLR 2026).
 
 ## What this adds
 
@@ -35,7 +35,7 @@ import { TurboQuant } from "turboquant-wasm";
 
 const tq = await TurboQuant.init({ dim: 1024, seed: 42 });
 
-// Compress a vector (~3 bits/dim, ~6x compression)
+// Compress a vector (~4.5 bits/dim, ~6x compression)
 const compressed = tq.encode(myFloat32Array);
 
 // Decode back
@@ -86,7 +86,7 @@ Encoding preserves inner products — verified by golden-value tests and distort
 ## Credits
 
 - [botirk38/turboquant](https://github.com/botirk38/turboquant) — original Zig implementation
-- Google's TurboQuant paper — algorithm design
+- [TurboQuant paper](https://arxiv.org/abs/2504.19874) (Google Research, ICLR 2026) — algorithm design
 
 ## License

@@ -12,7 +12,7 @@

TurboQuant 3D Gaussian Splatting

Proof-of-concept: general-purpose vector compression applied to SH coefficients in the browser.
Note: purpose-built formats (.ksplat, .spz) achieve better 3DGS compression. This demo shows TurboQuant works on real high-dimensional data via WASM. -
Scene data (~220MB) loaded from GitHub Releases.

+
Scene data (158 MB original + 65 MB compressed) loaded from GitHub.

@@ -12,7 +12,7 @@

turboquant-wasm

Shrink embedding vectors 6x. Search them without decompressing.

Vector compression has existed for years (FAISS, ScaNN, PQ), but they require - a training step on your dataset first. TurboQuant (Google Research, 2024) skips that — + a training step on your dataset first. TurboQuant (Google Research, ICLR 2026) skips that — just encode any vector immediately, no codebook, no training. Three lines of code: encode(), decode(), dot(). This npm package runs it in the browser via WASM with relaxed SIMD.
